title: Dialzara vs Radar: AI Phone Systems for Small Businesses Compared
summary: Dialzara is an AI receptionist for small businesses. Radar is an AI receptionist built specifically for tradespeople. Here's how they stack up.
context: Dialzara is a newer AI phone platform targeting SMBs broadly. Compare on: industry specificity (Dialzara is generic, Radar is trades-focused), integrations (does Dialzara connect to ServiceTitan/Jobber?), emergency handling (does it triage and dispatch?), customization (Radar lets you name your receptionist and trains on your specific business), pricing, and the human support model (Dialzara is self-serve, Solvr Labs provides a dedicated tech team). Be fair about what Dialzara does well — it's easy to set up and works for basic call answering. But for a plumber who needs emergency dispatch at midnight, Radar is in a different league.

title: Why Traditional Answering Services Don't Work for Tradespeople
summary: You're paying $300/month for someone to take a message and email it to you. Here's why that model is broken — and what actually works.
context: Tear down the traditional answering service model (AnswerConnect, PATLive, MAP Communications): they charge per minute, use scripts, can't access your calendar or CRM, can't dispatch, and create a game of phone tag. Every message is a delay. For a plumber with a burst pipe call at midnight, 'we'll pass along the message' means a lost customer. Compare this to Radar: AI answers instantly, books the job, dispatches the on-call tech, and alerts the owner — all before the caller hangs up. The message-taking model was built for offices, not field service. Position Radar as the modern replacement.

title: Goodcall vs Radar: Which AI Receptionist Actually Books Jobs?
summary: Goodcall is a popular AI phone agent. But can it dispatch your on-call tech at 2 AM? Let's compare.
context: Goodcall is an AI receptionist platform that's gotten traction with SMBs. Compare on: what happens after the call (Goodcall captures leads — but does it create jobs in ServiceTitan?), emergency handling, integration depth with field service tools, customization (naming your AI receptionist, training on your specific services/pricing/service area), simultaneous call handling during peak season, and pricing. Be fair — Goodcall is solid for basic businesses. But for a 5-person electrical company that needs dispatch, emergency triage, and CRM integration, Radar was built for exactly this.

title: How to Connect an AI Receptionist to ServiceTitan (Without the Headache)
summary: ServiceTitan is the gold standard for field service software. Here's how Radar plugs into it — and why most AI phone systems can't.
context: Technical but accessible post about integrating AI phone systems with ServiceTitan. Most AI answering services claim 'CRM integration' but it's just pushing a lead into a spreadsheet. Radar actually creates jobs, looks up customer records, checks technician availability, and dispatches — all through the ServiceTitan API during the call. Walk through the architecture: ElevenLabs voice -> Claude AI reasoning -> ServiceTitan API calls -> real-time dispatch. This is a unique technical moat. People searching 'ServiceTitan AI integration' or 'ServiceTitan phone system' should land here.

title: Jobber vs ServiceTitan vs Housecall Pro: Which Works Best with AI?
summary: All three are great field service platforms. But when you add an AI receptionist, the differences really matter.
context: Compare the big three field service platforms from the angle of AI integration. Which has the best API for real-time job creation? Which supports automated dispatch? How does Radar connect to each one? This post targets people already using these platforms who are searching for AI add-ons. Give a fair comparison of each platform's strengths, then show how Radar enhances all three. Position Solvr Labs as platform-agnostic — we integrate with whatever you already use. title: How Much Does a Missed Call Actually Cost Your Plumbing Business?
summary: We ran the numbers. For most plumbers, every missed call is $200-800 walking out the door. Here's the real math.
context: Data-driven post targeting 'missed calls plumbing business' and 'how much do missed calls cost'. Use real industry averages: plumbing avg ticket $350, 30-60% of inbound calls go unanswered, 85% of callers who reach voicemail don't leave a message, 75% of callers who can't reach you call a competitor. Walk through the ROI math: 10 missed calls/week × 40% conversion × $350 = $6,020/mo in lost revenue. Compare that to Radar at $149/mo. This is the rational business case. End with a link to the ROI calculator on the Radar page.

summary: Rosie AI is making waves in the AI phone space. Here's how it compares to Radar for plumbers, electricians, and HVAC techs.
context: Rosie AI is a newer AI receptionist specifically targeting home services — so this is a direct competitor comparison. title: Voice AI in 2026: What's Actually Good Now
summary: Voice AI has improved dramatically. Here's what's production-ready, what's still rough, and what I'm using for client projects.
context: Cover the current state of voice AI platforms — ElevenLabs, Play.ht, Deepgram, etc. Latency has dropped to near-real-time. Voices sound natural. But interruption handling is still tricky, multilingual support varies, and some platforms charge per minute. Compare from a builder's perspective. Mention that Radar uses ElevenLabs and why. Position Solvr Labs as having deep expertise in voice AI for service businesses.

title: Zapier vs n8n: Which Is Better for Service Business Automation?
summary: Zapier is easier. n8n is more powerful. Here's which one makes sense for your service business — and when to skip both.
context: Compare Zapier and n8n specifically for service business use cases: call routing, job creation, emergency alerts, calendar booking, CRM syncing. Zapier is great for simple workflows but gets expensive fast ($50-100+/mo) and has limits on complex logic. n8n is self-hosted, free, and handles complex branching — but requires technical setup. The real angle: for most tradespeople, you shouldn't be building automations at all. Radar and Taskline handle the complex stuff out of the box. Position Solvr Labs as the done-for-you alternative to DIY automation.

title: Supabase for Everything: Why It's My Go-To Backend
summary: PostgreSQL, auth, real-time, storage, edge functions — all in one platform. Here's how I use Supabase for client projects and Taskline.
context: Technical but accessible post about why Supabase is the best backend for solo developers and small teams. Row-level security for multi-tenant apps, real-time subscriptions for live dashboards, edge functions for webhooks. Compare to Firebase and raw Postgres setups. Include specific patterns from Taskline like the proposal storage and feedback loop. This post shows the technical depth behind Solvr Labs products.
